analogy: Think of "Gravitation" through the chapter's own opening images: Sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications Chapter 10 Sound is an everyday sensory experience that helps us become aware of our surroundings. Every day, we hear a variety of sounds in our surroundings, such as human voices, birds chirping, waves crashing on the seashore, leaves rustling,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10.1 Production of Sound, 186 Exploration|Grade 9, 10.1.1 Tuning fork, 10.2 Propagation of Sound. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Gravitation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications Chapter 10 Sound is an everyday sensory experience that helps us become aware of our surroundings.

The text explains that every day, we hear a variety of sounds in our surroundings, such as human voices, birds chirping, waves crashing on the seashore, leaves rustling, mobile phones ringing, vehicles honking, music, and the claps of thunder.

The text explains that you have learnt in Chapter 7 that sound is a form of energy.

The text explains that you have also learnt that energy can neither be created nor be destroyed.

The text explains that which form of energy gets converted to sound energy?

The text explains that how is sound produced and how does it reach our ears?

The text explains that in this chapter, we will try to find the answers to these questions.

The text explains that 10.1 Production of Sound You learnt a few ways to produce sound in your textbooks for arts in the earlier grades.

The text explains that grade 3 Bansuri I Chapter 9 Grade 4 Bansuri Chapter 13 Grade 5 Bansuri Chapter 12 Grade 6 Kriti I Chapter 7 Do you also remember learning about sonority earlier, a property of some metals where sound is produced when struck with an object (Fig.

The text explains that 10.1: Taal a musical instrument Grade 7 Curiosity Chapter 4 Think It Over y Two astronauts are repairing the arm of a space station together during a spacewalk.

The text explains that can they talk to each other and hear the sounds of metal clanking as they do on the Earth? y How do most bats use sound to locate their prey in the dark at night?

The text explains that sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications 185 Activity 10.1: Let us explore 1.

The text explains that take a cardboard box with one side open and a rubber band.

The text explains that stretch the rubber band across the open side of the box (Fig.

The text explains that holding the box steady with one hand, pluck the rubber band with a finger.

The text explains that pluck the rubber band again and watch it carefully.

The text explains that the object that produces sound is called the ‘source’ of the sound.

The text explains that 10.2: Vibrating rubber band produces sound Grade 4 Bansuri Chapter 13

The text explains that do you see waves forming on the surface of water?

The text explains that when the prong touches the surface of water, waves form on the surface of water, indicating that the prongs of the tuning fork are indeed vibrating (Fig.

The text explains that how does sound reach your ear from the source?

The text explains that sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications 187 2.

The text explains that sound propagates from its source to you through a medium.

The text explains that but suppose that there is no medium in the space between you and the source of the sound.

The text explains that hence, astronauts in spacesuits doing spacewalks cannot directly hear each other speak or hear sounds like two metal objects clanking together.

The text explains that reason (R): Sound requires a medium to travel.

The text explains that pause and Ponder 10.3 Sound Waves Sound needs a material medium to propagate but how does it propagate through that medium?

The text explains that this indicates that sound propagates in multiple directions from a source (in this case from the tuning fork).

The text explains that in general, the directions in which sound propagates may depend on the shape of the source.

The text explains that however, for simplicity, we will consider and illustrate sound as moving in only one direction.

The text explains that does the mark on the slinky move back and forth parallel to the direction of the disturbance?

The text explains that sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications 189 Fig.

The text explains that 10.9: Density of air in a tube with a piston (a) Piston not oscillating (average air density in the entire tube) (b) Forward motion of piston produces a compression (region of air with a higher density) C (c) Backward motion of piston produces a rarefaction (region of air with a lower density) C R (d) Oscillating piston produces alternate compressions and rarefactions C C R R You observe regions in the slinky where its turns are closer together than usual, and other regions where they are more spread out.

The text explains that however, the mark does not travel along the slinky, it only oscillates about its position of rest parallel to the direction of the disturbance.

The text explains that in our analogy, the closeness or spreading out of the turns of the slinky represents the higher or lower density of air through which sound is travelling.

The text explains that here, the oscillating piston is used as a simple and idealistic model of the source of sound to help understand how sound travels through air.

The text explains that when the piston is not oscillating, the air inside the tube has a certain uniform density, which we call the average density (Fig.

The text explains that as the piston moves forward, it pushes and displaces the nearby air particles in a forward direction, thereby compressing the air and increasing its density in a small region close to the piston (Fig.

The text explains that this region of air with higher density (compared to the average density) is called a compression (C).

The text explains that as a result, the higher density compression moves forward through the air even though the air particles themselves do not travel with it.

The text explains that as the piston moves backwards, the air particles move backwards in the direction of the piston and the air in a small region close to the piston becomes less dense (Fig.

The text explains that this region of air with lower density (compared to the average density) is called a rarefaction (R).

The text explains that as the piston oscillates in forward and backward directions, compressions and rarefactions are produced alternately (Fig.

The text explains that these travel away from the source, and a series of compression and rarefaction are produced.

The text explains that (ii) Signal your friend to burst one balloon.

The text explains that when you see the balloon burst, start the stopwatch.

The text explains that as soon as you hear the ‘pop’ sound of the bursting balloon, stop the timer and note down the reading.

The text explains that (iii) Repeat this experiment multiple times and take the average value of the times noted.

The text explains that (iv) Note the approximate distance between you and your friend using a map application on a mobile phone.

The text explains that (v) Divide the distance measured with the average time to get the average speed of sound.

The text explains that what value of speed did you get from the experiment?

The text explains that compare it with the speed of sound in air, which is typically about 346 m s–1 at 25 °C.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 10: Gravitation

prelims_traps: Do not treat 10.1 Production of Sound and 186 Exploration|Grade 9 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Gravitation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Gravitation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 10.1 Production of Sound and 186 Exploration|Grade 9 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Gravitation, which statement is supported by the source text about 10.2.1 Sound needs a medium to propagate?
options: From this activity, we conclude that sound is produced by vibrations.
Vibration refers to the periodic to and fro motion (oscillations) of an object.
Pulling a stretched string or striking a metal object makes them vibrate, which produces sound.
Likewise, when you blow through a bansuri (flute), vibration of the air inside the hollow pipe produces sound.
